# Related Work Draft

*Auto-generated prose draft built from narrative chains. Treat as a draft scaffold, not a final polished section.*


## Beat 1: Small-Town Campus Transportation & Coordination Gaps

Anchor paper: Javier Hernán Matas-Monroy et al. (2025), An importance-performance analysis of public transport to the university campus based on best-worst scaling.
It is the closest campus-specific transport paper in the set, so it can ground the section in university commuting needs before widening to the broader small-town/rural coordination gap.

Work on campus commuting and adjacent rural transport contexts documents that mobility gaps exist outside dense urban settings, where students and other riders face persistent mismatches between available service, trip flexibility, and accessibility needs. Key sources: Javier Hernán Matas-Monroy et al. (2025), An importance-performance analysis of public transport to the university campus based on best-worst scaling; Francisco Alonso et al. (2024), Public transportation means as seen by citizens: Approaching the case of the Dominican Republic; Shenura Jayatilleke et al. (2025), Advancing Rural Mobility: Identifying Operational Determinants for Effective Autonomous Road-Based Transit; Sivashankar Sivakanthan et al. (2024), Accessible Autonomous Transportation and Services: Design Considerations From the Perspective of Consumers and Providers.

Related rural-mobility studies further suggest that the access gap is not only a matter of missing vehicles, but also a coordination problem shaped by integration design, operating constraints, and unclear governance across transport options. Key sources: Tibor Rongen et al. (2025), Examining network governance of multimodal integration: A comparative study of rural mobility hubs in The Netherlands; Tibor Rongen et al. (2025), How to ensure sufficient access to public transport in rural areas? A comparative analysis of institutional designs for multimodal integration; Fabian Anzenhofer et al. (2025), Analyzing the impact of demand management in rural shared mobility-on-demand systems; Isabelle Wandenkolk et al. (2025), Department of Veterans Affairs’ Transportation System: Stakeholder Perspectives on the Current and Future System, Including Electric Autonomous Ride-Sharing Services.

This motivates looking beyond conventional transport provision alone: adjacent sharing-economy and carpooling work suggests that peer-mediated coordination may be worth designing in small communities, but only when trust, reputation, and participation are treated as first-order platform concerns. Key sources: E. Ert et al. (2015), Trust and reputation in the sharing economy: The role of personal photos in Airbnb; Liwei Li et al. (2020), The Effects of Online Trust-Building Mechanisms on Trust in the Sharing Economy: The Perspective of Providers; Markus Blut et al. (2024), Antecedents of Customer Participation on Sharing Platforms: A Meta‐analysis; Barbara Hartl et al. (2025), Creating a trusting environment in the sharing economy: Unpacking mechanisms for trust-building used by peer-to-peer carpooling platforms; Maryam Khodayari et al. (2025), The Sharing Economy: A Systematic Literature Review and Research Agenda.

Writing note: Keep the tone explicitly motivational. Say the small-town/campus rideshare gap exists, is documented, and motivates design; do not say it has been proved or quantified at scale. In paragraph 1, close with a short teaser to Beat 4 only: note that this motivation aligns with our later formative survey, where many Cornell participants describe Uber as expensive and sometimes unavailable (F1), but defer the full evidence and numbers to §4.1. Be candid that the Category A base here is proximate rather than perfect: it documents campus, rural, and underserved-setting transport frictions more directly than it documents Uber/Lyft failure in small-town university towns specifically. Likewise, use Category B only as a bridge to why peer or campus-scoped coordination platforms are worth considering; most of that literature comes from Airbnb/general sharing economy and one carpooling paper, so it should motivate design requirements rather than serve as direct evidence for later primary claims.


## Beat 2: Grassroots Coordination & Integrated Campus Platforms

Anchor paper: Tutaleni I. Asino et al. (2021), Bridging the Informal and Formal Learning Spaces with WhatsApp.
This is the closest campus-centered paper in the set: it documents WhatsApp as a bridge between formal and informal university activity, making it the cleanest anchor for a motivation section about student coordination channels before widening to international-student reliance and then to integrated-platform work.

Across university and international-student contexts, existing studies document that social media and messaging apps already mediate everyday navigation, support, and informal coordination, suggesting that peer-organized channels are a practical starting point rather than a new behavior to create. Key sources: Tutaleni I. Asino et al. (2021), Bridging the Informal and Formal Learning Spaces with WhatsApp; Zhiqiang Zhao et al. (2024), How Social Media as a Digital Marketing Strategy Influences Chinese Students' Decision to Study Abroad in the United States: A Model Analysis Approach; Luzi Shi et al. (2018), A Neglected Population: Media Consumption, Perceived Risk, and Fear of Crime Among International Students.; Lan Jin et al. (2021), Dilemmas and Coping Strategies of Chinese International Students’ Mental Health Problems: The Parents’ Perspectives; Sehyun Yun et al. (2022), Going Beyond the (Un)awakened Body: Arts-Based Collaborative Autoethnographic Inquiry of Korean Doctoral Students in the United States.

Beyond any single task, messaging-group research documents a recurring pattern: once a trusted chat channel exists, participants repurpose it for information exchange, mutual aid, identity work, and collective problem solving. Key sources: Tiziano Bonini et al. (2023), Cooperative affordances: How instant messaging apps afford learning, resistance and solidarity among food delivery workers; Unknown et al. (2018), Mobile instant messaging: New knowledge tools in global health?; Edith Shamir-Tixell et al. (2022), Volunteers during a crisis in Israel: a case study of spontaneous self-organized volunteer activity; Nakhi Mishol-Shauli et al. (2022), Smartphone Religious Networking: Negotiating Contested Identities within a Pious Community over WhatsApp; Zhenwu You et al. (2023), Residents' WeChat Group Use and Pro-Community Behavior in the COVID-19 Crisis: A Distal Mediating Role of Community Trust and Community Attachment.; Yasai Chen et al. (2024), Utilizing social media for community risk communication in megacities: analysing the impact of WeChat group information interaction and perception on communication satisfaction during the COVID-19 pandemic in Shanghai..

A separate but thinner literature suggests that bundling services into one application may reduce fragmentation, although the available examples are mostly city-scale or government-service systems and therefore provide only partial guidance for campus scope. Key sources: Marc Hasselwander et al. (2024), Local super apps in the 15-minute city: a new model for sustainable smart cities?; Yuyun Wulandari et al. (2024), Persepsi Publik Tentang Digitalisasi Layanan Pusaka Kemenag; Irwen Efendi et al. (2025), Implementasi Aplikasi Polri Super App Presisi Untuk Pelayanan Surat Keterangan Catatan Kepolisian (Skck) Di Polres Solok Selatan; Deandlles Christover et al. (2026), Navigating Digital Governance: Bridging the Gap in Developing SANTER as a Super-App for Samarinda's Smart City Transformation; Deandlles Christover et al. (2026), Navigating Digital Governance: Bridging the Gap in Developing SANTER as a Super-App for Samarinda's Smart City Transformation; Deandlles Christover et al. (2026), Navigating Digital Governance: Bridging the Gap in Developing SANTER as a Super-App for Samarinda's Smart City Transformation.

Because the student-oriented integration examples are narrow and the broader super-app references remain preliminary, the next step is to extract reusable design primitives that can connect grassroots coordination with lightweight modular integration. Key sources: Pramod Kumar Poladi et al. (2026), NeoPlace : A Smart Career Assistant for Modern Student Placements; Marc Hasselwander et al. (2024), Local super apps in the 15-minute city: a new model for sustainable smart cities?; Tiziano Bonini et al. (2023), Cooperative affordances: How instant messaging apps afford learning, resistance and solidarity among food delivery workers.

Writing note: Use this beat strictly as motivation, not as the direct evidence base for later primary claims. In paragraph 1, be explicit that the set documents messaging dependence and international-student reliance on mediated channels, but does not directly prove campus carpool behavior; then close the paragraph with a preview, not an argument, of local finding F2: 17/72 Mandarin respondents versus 1/15 English respondents reported using WeChat groups for carpool coordination. Prefer verbs such as document, indicate, and suggest. In paragraphs 3-4, describe the super-app literature as an initial inquiry rather than an established design space. Do not claim CampusRide is the first multi-module campus platform. A useful scope sentence near the end is that grassroots coordination is well documented, while


## Beat 3: Design Primitives: Identity, Safety, Rating Fairness, Rewards

Anchor paper: Miroslav Tushev et al. (2022), A Systematic Literature Review of Anti-Discrimination Design Strategies in the Digital Sharing Economy.
Among the candidate papers, this review most directly treats platform outcomes as products of design choices rather than isolated user behaviors, making it the best anchor for a neutral 'we distill' framework. It also helps frame rating fairness as one primitive while leaving room to place identity, safety, and rewards in parallel.

We distill two front-end design primitives that commonly structure platform trust: institutional identity and safety infrastructure, while noting that direct campus-specific identity evidence is thin here and safety work is concentrated in recent mobile-system literature. Key sources: E. Ert et al. (2015), Trust and reputation in the sharing economy: The role of personal photos in Airbnb; Barbara Hartl et al. (2025), Creating a trusting environment in the sharing economy: Unpacking mechanisms for trust-building used by peer-to-peer carpooling platforms; Thomas Neifer et al. (2023), Trust-Building in Peer-to-Peer Carsharing: Design Case Study for Algorithm-Based Reputation Systems; K.V. Divya et al. (2025), A Comprehensive Study on Mobile SOS and Personal Safety Applications Across Multiple Scenarios; Saddam F.Jagirdar et al. (2025), Android App for Women Safety; M.N. Aditya et al. (2026), A Comprehensive Web-Based Women Safety Application with Real-Time Tracking and AI-Powered Risk Assessment.

We then propose rating fairness as a separate primitive because peer reputation systems do more than summarize experience: they can also encode bias, retaliation, and platform power. Key sources: Miroslav Tushev et al. (2022), A Systematic Literature Review of Anti-Discrimination Design Strategies in the Digital Sharing Economy; Minsu Park et al. (2023), Fighting bias with bias: How same-race endorsements reduce racial discrimination on Airbnb; Vishal Kumar - et al. (2025), Enhancing the Reputation System in  Ridesharing: A Blockchain-Based Mutual Escrow and Cool-Off Period Approach; Tim Christiaens et al. (2025), Trust and power in Airbnb’s digital rating and reputation system; J. Martin Smit et al. (2026), Fairness Dynamics in Digital Economy Platforms with Biased Ratings; Maddalena Amendola et al. (2026), Gender Disparities in StackOverflow's Community-Based Question Answering: A Matter of Quantity versus Quality.

Finally, we treat rewards and gamification as a parallel motivational primitive that is commonly discussed for engagement and behavior change, without claiming that it is the dominant or validated lever in this setting. Key sources: Daniel Fernández Galeote et al. (2021), Gamification for climate change engagement: review of corpus and future agenda; Alireza Khakpour et al. (2020), Convergence of Gamification and Machine Learning: A Systematic Literature Review; Qingyu Zhang et al. (2023), Leveraging gamification technology to motivate environmentally responsible behavior: An empirical examination of Ant Forest; Martina Vacondio et al. (2025), Enhancing Sustainable Mobility Through Gamified Challenges: Evidence from a School-Based Intervention.

Writing note: Keep the tone definitional and parallel: 'we distill,' 'we propose,' 'commonly discussed.' Do not rank the four primitives in this beat. Be explicit that the institutional-identity strand is the weakest in the provided set: the best available evidence is adjacent identity/trust work, not direct .edu or campus-scope studies. Also note that the safety strand is mostly recent app/system literature, so it supports a practical design pattern (real-time location, SOS, safe routing) more than a long-established theoretical tradition. The fairness paragraph should emphasize that rating systems are socially and algorithmically shaped rather than neutral, but should not claim that any one mitigation fully solves bias. The rewards paragraph should present gamification as an optional motivational layer, not as a necessary ingredient.


## Beat 4: Formative Survey: Passenger-Side WTP & Motivations

Anchor paper: local:CornellCarpoolSurvey2026.
Formative Cornell Carpool Survey (N=117) is the primary-data source grounding passenger-side WTP and motivation findings. It is a pseudo-anchor because it is grey literature contributed by the authors, not a retrieved peer-reviewed paper.

The formative survey launched with N=117 and reached 50 fully complete responses; 82% of respondents reported Mandarin as their first language, which we disclose as scope rather than mask as bias.

Passengers report Uber as expensive (29/33) and often unavailable (24/33); across seven asked-for features, all cross the 50% WTP threshold, with real-time location (70.1%), .edu verification (65.9%), and emergency SOS (64.4%) as the three highest.

The motivation structure separates into two tiers: financial at 63.6%, then gamification at 48.3% followed by social (45.6%) and environmental (44.5%); gamification as the second-tier motivator informs the platform's decision to keep points auxiliary rather than primary in §5.

Writing note: Beat 4 is §4.1 passenger-side survey findings (primary_data). The anchor is the pseudo-anchor local:CornellCarpoolSurvey2026 (N=117 formative survey). The spine references findings (F1/F3/F4) and section labels, not paperIds. Narrative: (1) recap N=117 / 50 complete / 82% Mandarin scope disclosure, (2) transport-gap quantification (F1), (3) WTP safety-feature ranking (F3: location 70.1%, .edu 65.9%, SOS 64.4%), (4) motivation structure (F4: financial 63.6%, gamification 48.3%, social 45.6%, environmental 44.5%). NO inferential statistics, NO p-values, NO 'significantly'. Use: we observe, reports indicate, median value is. Disclose 82% Mandarin-native as scope, not mask as bias. (Primary-data / artifact beat: chain assembled from manual_core_inclusions.primary_anchors; contextualizing literature is listed as supporting rather than spine.)


## Beat 5: Formative Survey: Driver-Side Tolerance & Rating-Fairness Asymmetry

Anchor paper: Nura Jabagi et al. (2024), Fairness in Algorithmic Management: Bringing Platform-Workers into the Fold.
Closest available algorithmic-management fairness anchor in the candidate set. It offers a worker-perception lens for interpreting the local finding that unfair ratings were least tolerated, without implying that a small campus peer-driving sample reproduces labor-platform dynamics at scale.

In our N=30 campus driver sample, tolerance was highest for route change (44.8%) and lateness (42.7%), lower for destination change (33.0%), and counterintuitively lowest for unfair ratings (26.6%); we treat that pattern as resonating with algorithmic-management concerns about evaluative fairness, not as a replication of large-platform labor findings. Key sources: Nura Jabagi et al. (2024), Fairness in Algorithmic Management: Bringing Platform-Workers into the Fold; Anne Keegan et al. (2025), Algorithmic Management in Organizations? From Edge Case to Center Stage; Lan‐Xia Zhang et al. (2025), How Does Algorithmic Control Affect the Work Engagement of Gig Workers? The Role of Perceived Algorithmic Fairness and Psychological Contract; M Arathi et al. (2025), An Empirical Analysis of Algorithmic Control and Worker Perceptions in the Gig Economy.

Key sources: Tim Christiaens et al. (2025), Trust and power in Airbnb’s digital rating and reputation system; Miroslav Tushev et al. (2022), A Systematic Literature Review of Anti-Discrimination Design Strategies in the Digital Sharing Economy.


## Beat 6: CampusRide Multi-Module Platform Design with Carpool Deep-Dive

Anchor paper: local:CampusRideSystem2026.
CampusRide multi-module platform is the artifact anchor for the core contribution. It is a pseudo-anchor because the system itself is the evidence, not a peer-reviewed paper about it; the system is described via its implementation documentation.

CampusRide is a Vue 3 + Express + Supabase + Socket.IO platform integrating six modules (carpool, marketplace, activities, groups, messaging, points) that share four design primitives from Beat 3.

Each module reuses the platform's shared .edu identity layer and messaging infrastructure; only carpool fully exercises all four primitives at maximum intensity, which is why we select it for deep-dive analysis.

The carpool deep-dive operationalizes four design decisions motivated by survey findings: .edu identity verification (F3), safety skeleton with real-time location and SOS (F3), bidirectional rating with fairness considerations (F5), and gamification kept as a secondary incentive (F4).

Writing note: Beat 6 is §5 CampusRide platform (artifact). The anchor is the pseudo-anchor local:CampusRideSystem2026. The spine references module names ('module:carpool', 'module:marketplace', etc.) and finding keys ('finding:F3', 'finding:F4', 'finding:F5'), not real paperIds. Narrative: (1) §5.1 platform overview with 6-module x 4-primitive matrix, (2) §5.2–5.7 each module gets one subsection at overview level, (3) §5.8 carpool deep-dive with four design decisions (identity, safety, rating fairness, gamification) each explicitly motivated by a survey finding. Use: we designed, we implemented, motivated by, in response to finding X. AVOID: effective, successful, proves, validates. Acknowledge: no deployment data; six-module claim acknowledges only carpool is deep-dived. (Primary-data / artifact beat: chain assembled from manual_core_inclusions.primary_anchors; contextualizing literature is listed as supporting rather than spine.)


## Beat 7: Adversarial Scoping: Formalization Risk, Sample Skew, No Deployment

Anchor paper: Anne Keegan et al. (2025), Algorithmic Management in Organizations? From Edge Case to Center Stage.
Best broad honesty anchor for opening the section: it treats algorithmic management as a general organizational risk rather than an edge case, which lets the beat begin by admitting that a coordination platform may reproduce known control and regulatory harms rather than assuming benign mediation.

A first scope limit is that turning informal campus coordination into app logic may reproduce the control, surveillance, and autonomy losses documented in algorithmic-management research. Key sources: Anne Keegan et al. (2025), Algorithmic Management in Organizations? From Edge Case to Center Stage; James Duggan et al. (2023), Algorithmic <scp>HRM</scp> control in the gig economy: The app‐worker perspective; Hanna Kinowska et al. (2022), Influence of algorithmic management practices on workplace well-being – evidence from European organisations; Muhammed Ali et al. (2024), Cogs in the code: Applying labor process theory in algorithmic management of platform-mediated gig work.

We also acknowledge that our survey sample is skewed, and prior platform research suggests that fairness and burden are patterned by social position rather than captured well by a single average-user account. Key sources: Miroslav Tushev et al. (2022), A Systematic Literature Review of Anti-Discrimination Design Strategies in the Digital Sharing Economy; Al James et al. (2023), Platform work‐lives in the gig economy: Recentering work–family research; Isabella Stingl et al. (2024), Becoming "Platform Workers": A Biographical Exploration of Temporary Visa and Worker Subjectivities in the Gig Economy.; Ajeet Kumar Pankaj et al. (2024), Gig workers in precarious life: The trajectory of exploitation, insecurity, and resistance.

Because CampusRide was not deployed and studied longitudinally, we cannot infer how users would actually interpret, contest, or adapt to its rules once the system operates in everyday practice. Key sources: Matthias Weber et al. (2023), Opaque Overwatch: How Food-Delivery Workers Make Sense of Algorithmic Management in the Gig Economy; Nura Jabagi et al. (2024), Fairness in Algorithmic Management: Bringing Platform-Workers into the Fold; Lan‐Xia Zhang et al. (2025), How Does Algorithmic Control Affect the Work Engagement of Gig Workers? The Role of Perceived Algorithmic Fairness and Psychological Contract.

Relatedly, a campus-affiliation check is only an identity boundary: it does not by itself establish trustworthy behavior, competent driving, or fair governance of ratings and visibility. Key sources: Tim Christiaens et al. (2025), Trust and power in Airbnb’s digital rating and reputation system; Minsu Park et al. (2023), Fighting bias with bias: How same-race endorsements reduce racial discrimination on Airbnb; J. Martin Smit et al. (2026), Fairness Dynamics in Digital Economy Platforms with Biased Ratings.

Finally, the cross-module points layer may invite gaming, because visible scoring systems often encourage users to optimize for the metric or manipulate the evaluation process rather than the underlying coordination goal. Key sources: EunJeong Cheon et al. (2025), Fulfillment of the Work Games: Warehouse Workers' Experiences with Algorithmic Management; Vishal Kumar - et al. (2025), Enhancing the Reputation System in  Ridesharing: A Blockchain-Based Mutual Escrow and Cool-Off Period Approach; Maddalena Amendola et al. (2026), Gender Disparities in StackOverflow's Community-Based Question Answering: A Matter of Quantity versus Quality.

Writing note: Keep the tone explicitly concessive: use formulations such as 'may reproduce,' 'we acknowledge,' and 'scope-limited.' Do not convert any paragraph into a defense or mitigation claim. Paragraph 2 should directly name the sample skew (82% Mandarin-native; English-native students N=15) and admit that the cited evidence is indirect: it shows demographic heterogeneity in platform outcomes, not language-specific campus carpool behavior. Paragraph 3 must state that no real-world deployment compared the four design decisions, so no claims should be made about improved uptake, safety, fairness, or retention. Paragraph 4 should separate identity verification from competence, conduct, and governance. Paragraph 5 should say that points may invite gaming or fabricated activity; the support is adjacent platform evidence, which should be acknowledged as analogous rather than definitive.
